figures.caption: Table 6: Comparison of MoE-style and LoRA-based composition methods for model merging. Memory overhead is relative to base model size.
Table 3: Comparison of weight-space averaging and geometric interpolation methods for model merging.
Table 4: Comparison of task vector arithmetic and sparsification-enhanced methods for model merging.

Table 1: Overview of major open-weight foundation models (Brown et al., 2020; Team, 2024b) commonly used in model merging research. These models serve as the base for fine-tuning and subsequent merging experiments.

Figure 1: Overview of the model merging pipeline. Task-specific models {θ1, . . . , θK}, obtained by fine-tuning a shared pretrained checkpoint θ0, are combined via one of several strategies (Sections 3–5) into a single model θmerged, which is then evaluated on all source tasks. Gray vertical bars represent the many-to-many mapping between model inputs and merging strategies.

Figure 3: Four fundamental task vector operations. (a) Addition combines multiple task vectors into a single model. (b) Negation subtracts a task vector to remove unwanted behavior. (c) Scaling controls task influence via λ. (d) Analogy transfers the relationship between two tasks.

Table 5: Unified comparison of representative model merging methods. Data-Free: no training/calibration data required. Interference: explicit handling of parameter conflicts. Shared Init: requires common pretrained initialization.

Figure 4: Evolution of model merging techniques. The field progressed from foundational loss landscape theory (2018–2020), through weight-space averaging (2022) and interferenceaware task vectors (2023), to adaptive/evolutionary optimization and standardized ecosystem tooling (2024–2025). Arrows indicate the conceptual flow and inheritance of ideas across phases.
Figure 2: The FUSE taxonomy. Foundations (§2): why merging works. Unification (§3–§5): how models are combined. Scenarios (§6): where merging applies. Ecosystem (§7): what supports deployment.
Table 2: Comparison of theoretical foundations and concepts in model merging.
